 {"id":"bd-5s0r.5","title":"Cross-crate integration: write-back cache triggers RaptorQ symbol refresh","description":"# Cross-crate integration: write-back cache triggers RaptorQ symbol refresh

## GOAL
Wire the write-back cache flush path to trigger RaptorQ symbol refresh for modified block groups. This is the critical cross-crate integration between ffs-block (cache), ffs-repair (RaptorQ), and the flush daemon.

## BACKGROUND
Currently bd-820 (RaptorQ symbol refresh on dirty group flush) exists but is not wired into the write-back cache flush path. When FlushDaemon (bd-5s0r.2) flushes dirty blocks, it must notify the repair subsystem that those block groups need symbol recalculation.

## DELIVERABLES
1. FlushDaemon::flush_batch() calls repair::mark_groups_dirty(groups) after successful disk write
2. Repair subsystem queues symbol recalculation for dirty groups
3. Integration test: write -> flush -> verify symbols updated

## TESTS (REQUIRED)
1. Unit: flush_batch marks correct groups dirty
2. Unit: repair subsystem receives group notifications
3. Integration: full write -> flush -> symbol refresh -> corruption -> recovery cycle

## LOGGING REQUIREMENTS
- Flush triggers refresh (debug): group_ids, block_count
- Symbol refresh queued (debug): group_id, priority
- Symbol refresh complete (info): group_id, duration_ms, symbol_count

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. Every flushed block group eventually gets symbol refresh
2. No symbol staleness > 2 scrub intervals
3.  {"id":"bd-5s0r.6","title":"Add unit tests for write-back cache DirtyTracker and FlushDaemon","description":"# Add unit tests for write-back cache DirtyTracker and FlushDaemon

## GOAL
Provide unit test coverage for the Write-Back Cache epic (bd-5s0r). Currently has E2E test (bd-5s0r.4) but NO unit tests for DirtyTracker invariants or FlushDaemon behavior.

## TEST PLAN

### DirtyTracker Tests
1. Mark block dirty: dirty flag set, dirty count incremented
2. Mark block clean after flush: dirty flag cleared, count decremented
3. Dirty list ordering: oldest dirty block is first
4. Double-mark is idempotent
5. Mark clean on non-dirty block is no-op
6. Dirty ratio calculation is correct

### FlushDaemon Tests
7. Flush batch selects oldest dirty blocks first
8. Flush batch size respects configuration
9. Flush under backpressure: batch size reduced
10. Failed flush retries (block stays dirty)
11. fsync flushes all dirty blocks for specific inode
12. Daemon shutdown completes in-flight flush

### Eviction Coordination Tests
13. Dirty block cannot be evicted without flush
14. Flush failure prevents eviction (block stays in cache)
15. Clean block can be evicted immediately

## TESTS (REQUIRED)
All 15 tests above, deterministic, mock I/O layer.

## LOGGING REQUIREMENTS
- Test failures log full dirty tracker state

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. All 15 tests pass
2. Dirty-block-never-evicted invariant verified
3.  {"id":"bd-g1ue","title":"Harden ffs-dir test coverage with property-based and boundary tests","description":"## What
Expand ffs-dir from 49 tests to 70+ with property-based tests (proptest) and additional
boundary/corruption tests.

## Why
ffs-dir has the lowest test count (49) of any non-trivial crate. Directory operations are
safety-critical: a bug in add_entry/remove_entry can corrupt the entire directory structure.
The crate has proptest as a dev-dependency but no property-based tests yet.

## Current Coverage (49 tests)
- init_dir_block: 5 tests (basic, tail, sizes, inode numbers)
- add_entry: 10 tests (split, reuse, enospc, reject, max name, types)
- remove_entry: 7 tests (coalesce, first entry, nonexistent, overlap)
- htree: 8 tests (insert, find, remove, edge cases)
- compute_dx_hash: 3 tests (deterministic, seeds, names)
- Multi-operation: 3 tests (fill, cycle, alternating)
- Corruption detection: 3 tests (rec_len zero, misaligned, exceeds block)

## Gaps to Fill
1. Property-based tests (proptest):
   - proptest_add_remove_roundtrip: random names (1..255 bytes), random inodes,
     add then remove → block parseable, entry gone
   - proptest_add_never_exceeds_block: random sequence of adds → never writes past limit
   - proptest_init_parse_roundtrip: random self_ino, parent_ino, reserved_tail →
     init_dir_block → parse_dir_block → exactly . and .. with correct inodes
   - proptest_htree_insert_always_sorted: random hash sequence → entries always sorted

2. Boundary tests:
   - add_entry with exactly 0 bytes of slack (entry fits perfectly, no split needed)
   - remove_entry of last entry in block (rec_len extends to block end)
   - init_dir_block with reserved_tail = block_size - 24 (minimum viable)
   - add_entry with name at every alignment boundary (1,2,3,4,5,8,12,16 bytes)

3. Corruption resilience:
   - add_entry with rec_len pointing past block boundary
   - remove_entry with circular rec_len chain (entry points back to itself)
   - add_entry to block of all zeros (no valid first entry)

4. Stress tests:
   - Fill 4096-byte block completely, verify parse_dir_block recovers all entries
   - Add 100 entries, remove 50, add 50 more, verify final state

## Implementation Plan
Add to the existing #[cfg(test)] module in crates/ffs-dir/src/lib.rs.
proptest is already a dev-dependency of ffs-dir.

## Test Plan
- cargo test -p ffs-dir --lib → all pass, count >= 70
- proptest cases: 128 per property test
- No flakes in 3 consecutive runs

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] 4+ property-based tests added
- [ ] 5+ boundary tests added
- [ ] 3+ corruption resilience tests added
- [ ] 2+ stress tests added
- [ ] Total test count >= 70
- [ ] Zero test flakes","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-06T16:18:00.522669212Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-06T21:42:36.913247920Z","closed_at":"2026-04-06T21:42:36.912968691Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"bd-k9zm","title":"Apply fast commit operations to filesystem metadata during mount","description":"BACKGROUND: ext4 fast commit (FC) is a lightweight journal mechanism that records individual filesystem operations (create, link, unlink, add_range, del_range, inode update) instead of full block snapshots. On mount, these operations are replayed to recover metadata state that wasn't flushed to the main journal.

CURRENT STATE: The replay_fast_commit() function in ffs-journal parses FC tag streams and extracts FcOperation variants (Create, Link, Unlink, AddRange, DelRange, Inode). The mount-time path (maybe_collect_ext4_fast_commit_evidence in ffs-core) reads these operations and stores them as Ext4FastCommitReplayEvidence — purely observational.

FEATURE_PARITY.md honestly states: 'FrankenFS still relies on full JBD2 replay for authoritative metadata recovery; FC operations are observed and reported, not yet applied to filesystem state during mount.'

WHAT NEEDS TO HAPPEN:
1. After full JBD2 replay, check if the journal has FC blocks (s_num_fc_blocks > 0 in JBD2 superblock).
2. Parse the FC tag stream from the journal's FC tail region.
3. For each committed FC transaction (HEAD...TAIL pair):
   a. Create: allocate inode, add dir entry to parent.
   b. Link: add dir entry to parent (inode already exists).
   c. Unlink: remove dir entry from parent.
   d. AddRange: insert extent mapping for the inode.
   e. DelRange: remove extent mapping range.
   f. Inode: update inode metadata (size, timestamps, etc.).
4. Write the updated metadata blocks to disk.
5. Clear the FC area in the journal superblock.

COMPLEXITY: High. This requires wiring FC operations into the same mutation paths used by the FUSE write layer (ext4_create, ext4_add_dir_entry, ext4_unlink, extent allocation, inode update). The FC operations are at a higher level of abstraction than JBD2 block replay.

KEY RISKS:

 {"id":"bd-qpt5","title":"Add SIMD-accelerated checksum computation","description":"# Add SIMD-accelerated checksum computation

## GOAL
Accelerate CRC32C and BLAKE3 checksum computation using SIMD instructions.

## BACKGROUND (Extreme Software Optimization)
Checksum computation is on the hot path:
- Every block read/write verifies checksum
- ARC cache hits still verify (trust but verify)
- RaptorQ symbols have checksums

SIMD can provide 4-16x speedup depending on instruction set.

## TECHNICAL REQUIREMENTS

### 1. CRC32C Acceleration
```rust
pub fn crc32c_simd(data: &[u8], seed: u32) -> u32 {
    #[cfg(target_arch = "x86_64")]
    {
        if is_x86_feature_detected!("sse4.2") {
            return crc32c_sse42(data, seed);
        }
    }
    #[cfg(target_arch = "aarch64")]
    {
        if is_aarch64_feature_detected!("crc") {
            return crc32c_arm_crc(data, seed);
        }
    }
    crc32c_fallback(data, seed)
}
```

### 2. BLAKE3 (Already SIMD)
- blake3 crate already uses SIMD
- Ensure proper feature flags enabled
- Benchmark to confirm

### 3. Batch Checksum
```rust
/// Compute checksums for multiple blocks in parallel
pub fn batch_checksum(blocks: &[&[u8]], algo: ChecksumAlgo) -> Vec<u32> {
    // Use rayon for multi-block parallelism
    // Use SIMD within each block
}
```

### 4. Feature Detection at Runtime
- Detect CPU features once at startup
- Store in global (or thread-local)
- Dispatch to appropriate implementation

## TESTS
1. Unit: SIMD matches fallback results
2. Unit: All supported CPUs tested (via CI matrix)
3. Benchmark: Before/after comparison
4. Property: Random data, SIMD == fallback

## LOGGING
- Feature detection (info): detected SIMD capabilities
- Batch checksum (trace): block count, duration

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. Correctness: SIMD == fallback for all inputs
2. Performance: >2x speedup on supported CPUs
3. Graceful fallback on unsupported CPUs
4. No unsafe code (use safe SIMD wrappers)

## NOTES
Use existing crates:
- crc32c crate has SIMD support
- blake3 crate is already optimized
Main work is ensuring proper feature flags and benchmarking.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T02:55:14.096946312Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-17T15:05:05.163955333Z","closed_at":"2026-02-17T15:05:05.163936237Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["optimization","perf","simd"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-qpt5","depends_on_id":"bd-3ib","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:57:00.533583752Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-qpt5","depends_on_id":"bd-3ib.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:53:29.930946133Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":207,"issue_id":"bd-qpt5","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Alien uplift contract (auto-applied via extreme-software-optimization + alien-artifact-coding + alien-graveyard).
